issue: Middle East Weapons of Mass Destruction Free Zone and Israel

entries.position_summary: Russia strongly supports establishing a WMD-free zone in the Middle East, framing it as a top non-proliferation priority. It cites the 2024 BRICS Kazan Declaration endorsing accelerated implementation of relevant resolutions and has circulated a working document to advance the issue.
entries.quote: Unofficial translation STATEMENT by the Delegation of the Russian Federation at the Third Session of the Preparatory Committee for the 11th Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons (Middle East zone free of nuclear weapons and other weapons of mass destruction) (New York, 6 May 2025) Mr. Chair, The Russian Federation has consistently supported the idea of establishing a zone free of nuclear weapons and other weapons of mass destruction (a WMD-free zone) in the Middle East. This issue has traditionally been one of our top foreign policy priorities related to nuclear non-proliferation. This stance was reflected in particular in the Kazan Declaration "Strengthening Multilateralism for Just Global Development and Security" adopted by the BRICS Summit on 23 October 2024 and emphasizing the efforts aiming at accelerating the implementation of the resolutions on the establishment of a zone free of nuclear weapons and other weapons of mass destruction in the Middle East. Ahead of this session of the Preparatory Committee, we circulated a pertinent working document and invite delegations to study it.

entries.position_summary: Russia urges the 2026 NPT Review Conference to recommend that all Middle Eastern states and observers participate in the UN Conference on establishing a WMD-free zone, aiming to accelerate tangible progress toward its early creation.
entries.quote: 15. Taking into account the foregoing, the Russian Federation proposes that the participants in the 2026 Review Conference recommend that all countries of the Middle East without exception, as well as States invited as observers, participate in the United Nations Conference on a WMD-free zone in order to make more active and tangible progress towards the early establishment of such a zone in the region. 25-03566 3/3

entries.position_summary: Russia reaffirms support for establishing a Middle East zone free of nuclear and other WMD as mandated by the 1995 NPT resolution. As a co-sponsor, it acknowledges its responsibility and commits to continue efforts to achieve this goal.
entries.quote: Mr. Chairman, The issue of establishing a zone free from nuclear weapons and other weapons of mass destruction (WMD-free zone) in the Middle East as envisaged by the resolution of the 1995 NPT Review and Extension Conference, has always played special role in the NPT context. Russia, which co-sponsored the resolution, is fully conscious of the responsibility this status implies and intends to continue making all the necessary efforts to reach the objectives of this resolution.

entries.position_summary: The country reaffirms the 1995 NPT Middle East resolution and supports creating a Middle East zone free of nuclear and other WMD. It welcomes the ongoing annual Conference on a Middle East WMD-Free Zone (launched in 2018), participates as an observer, and urges engagement by all regional states and all nuclear-weapon states.
entries.quote: We reaffirm the significance and relevance of the 1995 resolution on the Middle East adopted at the NPT Review and Extension Conference. As one of its co-sponsors, we support the efforts to establish a zone free of nuclear weapons and other weapons of mass destruction (WMD-free zone) in the region. We welcome the process of annual sessions of the Conference on a Middle East WMD-Free zone, which was launched in 2018 and in which Russia takes part as an observer. It is important that all the countries of the region and all nuclear-weapon States engaged in its work.

entries.position_summary: Russia supports convening a UN-led conference to establish a WMD-free zone in the Middle East, backing UNGA decision 73/546 as a concrete step forward. It blames the United States, United Kingdom, and Canada for obstructing progress and frames the initiative as aligned with the UN’s peace and security mandate.
entries.quote: 7. More than 20 years without any tangible progress in implementing the 1995 resolution has predictably led to growing discontent among the Arab countries of the region. In view of the stalemate, the League of Arab States initiated returning the WMD-free zone issue to “inside the walls” of the United Nations and holding of a relevant conference, facilitated by the Secretary-General of the United Nations. The Russian Federation supported that step, based primarily on the mandate of the United Nations to maintain international peace and security, but also in view of the blatant sabotage of that process by Washington, London and Ottawa, which had aligned itself with them. As a result, the General Assembly adopted decision 73/546 of 23 December 2018 on convening a United Nations conference on the establishment of WMD-free zone in the Middle East, marking the first practical step towards the establishment of a WMD-free zone since 1974.

entries.position_summary: Russia expresses consistent and strong support for establishing a WMD-free zone in the Middle East, calling it a high foreign policy priority. It views the zone as an effective means to strengthen regional security and the broader non-proliferation regime, benefiting all states in and beyond the region.
entries.quote: STATEMENT by the Delegation of the Russian Delegation at the First Session of the Preparatory Committee for the 11th Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons on the Establishment of a Zone Free of Nuclear and Other Weapons of Mass Destruction in the Middle East (Vienna, 7 August 2023) Mr. Chair, The Russian Federation has consistently supported the idea of establishing a zone free of nuclear and other weapons of mass destruction (a WMD-free zone) in the Middle East. This issue has traditionally been high on the list of our foreign policy priorities, since we regard it as an effective way of strengthening regional security and the international non-proliferation regime. The creation of such a zone would be in the interests of all without exception States in the Middle East and beyond.

entries.position_summary: Russia underscores its responsibility as a co-sponsor of the 1995 Resolution on a Middle East WMD-free zone and insists the issue remain on the NPT review agenda until the resolution’s objectives are fully realized.
entries.quote: Russia was one of the co-sponsors of that document. We are fully aware of the responsibility that this status entails for us and believe that the WMD-free zone issue should remain on the agenda of the NPT review process until the goals and objectives of the Resolution are fully achieved.

entries.position_summary: Supports convening and advancing a Middle East WMD-free zone, citing its own initiatives (2012–2013 consultations) and proposal at the 2015 NPT Review Conference. Blames the United States, United Kingdom, and Canada for blocking consensus, which it says stalled progress.
entries.quote: 2 subsequent efforts to convene a conference on a Middle East WMD-free zone with the participation of all States of the region, including the process of informal consultations in Glion and Geneva in 2012-2013 initiated by our country. There was also the 2015 NPT Review Conference, when Russia's proposal served as a basis for the Middle East section of the outcome document. Unfortunately, the emerging consensus was thwarted by the United States, the United Kingdom and Canada — of whom the former two had co-sponsored the Resolution of 1995 — which essentially precluded the NPT forum from making any progress towards the establishment of a WMD-free zone for years to come.

entries.position_summary: The country emphasizes that inclusive regional dialogue, including Israel, is essential to advance a Middle East WMD-free zone. It notes that the 2013–2014 Glion-Geneva consultations nearly agreed on a conference but ultimately did not produce concrete results.
entries.quote: 5. In 2013–2014, for the first time since the Madrid process of the early 1990s, five rounds of informal consultations on the possible convening of a conference on a WMD-free zone were held in Glion and Geneva (Switzerland), in which Israel also participated. At a certain point, the participants almost came to an agreement on the date of a future conference and its organizational arrangements. Unfortunately, however, it was not possible to come up with concrete solutions. Although the Glion- Geneva process did not yield practical results, it demonstrated that dialogue among the countries of the region, including Israel, is possible, and that meaningful progress towards the establishment of a WMD-free zone can only be achieved in this way.

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes the failure to hold the 2012 conference on a Middle East WMD-free zone and accuses the UK, US, and Canada of blocking progress at the 2015 NPT Review Conference. It implies support for establishing the zone and disapproval of those obstructing it.
entries.quote: 6. Nevertheless, the 2012 conference never took place and any further attempts under the Treaty to move towards establishing a WMD-free zone were blocked at the 2015 Review Conference by the United Kingdom and the United States, as well as by Canada, which joined them.

entries.position_summary: The country welcomes and supports the annual UNGA-launched Conference on a Middle East WMD-Free Zone, participates as an observer, and notes substantive progress and decisions. It expresses a positive view of the process.
entries.quote: We welcome the process of annual sessions of the Conference on a Middle East WMD-Free zone, which was launched by the UN General Assembly in 2018 and in which our country takes part as an observer. Many important substantive discussions have already taken place there, and many specific decisions have been made.

entries.position_summary: Russia asserts it has played a leading role in advancing a Middle East WMD-free zone, citing the 2010 NPT Action Plan, its 2012–2013 informal consultations, and a 2015 proposal that underpinned the RevCon outcome’s Middle East section. It contends that an emerging consensus was blocked by the United States, the United Kingdom, and Canada.
entries.quote: The Russian side has made a significant contribution to the establishment of a WMD-free zone at every stage of this process. In addition to the aforementioned Resolution, it is necessary to note the 2010 NPT Review Conference Action Plan and subsequent efforts to convene a conference on a Middle East WMD-free zone with the participation of all States of the region, including the Russia-initiated process of informal consultations in Glion and Geneva in 2012-2013. It is also the 2015 NPT Review Conference, when it was the Russian proposal that formed the basis for the Middle East section of the outcome document. Unfortunately, the emerging consensus was then thwarted by the United States, the United Kingdom and Canada, which

entries.position_summary: Supports and will participate as an observer in the 4th UN Conference on a Middle East WMD-free zone, and encourages China, the UK, and France to join. Emphasizes a constructive, respectful observer role that engages in good faith at the request of forum participants.
entries.quote: We are grateful to the organizers for the invitation to participate once again as an observer in the fourth session of the UN Conference on a Middle East WMD-free zone scheduled for 13-17 November 2023. We are ready once again to send Russian representatives to New York. We consider it crucial that, as in previous years, China, the United Kingdom and France join the work. The status of observer at this forum is important to us. We are convinced that, when acting in this capacity, it is necessary to respect the hospitality of the forum hosts and not to lecture them. Observers are required to have a constructive attitude and engage in the process in good faith, responding to the request of the forum participants themselves. For our part, we will continue to follow this logic.

entries.position_summary: Urges Israel to reconsider its negative stance and engage in the process to establish a Middle East WMD-free zone, and welcomes that the UN Conference keeps the door open for Israel’s participation.
entries.quote: We call on Israel that continues to evade the process of the establishment of a Middle East WMD-free zone to reconsider their negative attitude towards it. The participants in the UN Conference wisely leave the door open for Israel's engagement

entries.position_summary: The delegation prioritizes establishing a Middle East WMD-Free Zone and stresses that progress hinges on the involvement of Israel and the United States. It underscores the special responsibility of these co-sponsors to implement the 1995 Middle East resolution, which it considers an integral part of the 1995 NPT agreements.
entries.quote: Mr. Chair, The issue of establishing a Middle East Weapons of Mass Destruction Free Zone occupies a special place in the NPT framework. The key to successful progress on this track is the involvement of Israel and the United States, which, as co-sponsors of the 1995 resolution on the Middle East, bear special responsibility for the implementation of its provisions. We view this resolution as an integral element of the agreements reached in 1995 to strengthen the non-proliferation regime.

entries.position_summary: The country supports establishing a WMD-free (and historically nuclear-weapon-free) zone in the Middle East, viewing it as beneficial for regional and global security. It assesses that near-term implementation is unlikely under current conditions but commits to supporting the process as much as possible.
entries.quote: Mr. Chair, During the half a century that have passed since the UN General Assembly adopted the first resolution on a nuclear-weapon-free zone in the Middle East in 1974, a great deal of effort has been made to make a ME WMDFZ reality. Such a zone would considerably strengthen international peace and security both within the region and beyond. Unfortunately, in the current circumstances the chances of practical implementation of this initiative in the near future are slim. We see our task here in supporting this process as much as possible.

entries.position_summary: Russia strongly supports establishing a Middle East WMD-free zone as envisaged by the 1995 NPT resolution, seeing it as vital for regional stability and non-proliferation. It participates as an observer in the annual UN conference on the zone and urges engagement by all invited states.
entries.quote: Russia has consistently supported efforts to establish a zone free from nuclear weapons and other weapons of mass destruction (WMD-free zone) in the Middle East as envisaged by the resolution of the 1995 NPT Review and Extension Conference. Such a zone would serve as an effective means of strengthening regional stability and the nuclear non-proliferation regime for the benefit of all the States of the Middle East without exception. As an observer State, Russia contributes to the annual UN Conference on the Establishment of a Middle East WMD-free zone. It is vitally important to ensure the engagement of all the invited States in the work of the Conference.

entries.position_summary: The country marks the 50th anniversary of UNGA resolution 3263 and expresses strong support for establishing a nuclear-weapon-free zone in the Middle East, noting sustained efforts via the NPT, the UN, and the IAEA. It argues that achieving this goal would significantly enhance regional and global peace and security.
entries.quote: 1. December 2024 marked 50 years since the adoption by the General Assembly, at the initiative of Iran and Egypt, of resolution 3263 (XXIX) entitled “Establishment of a nuclear-weapon-free zone in the region of the Middle East”. Over the past half- century, many efforts have been made by the countries of the region, both through the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons and at the United Nations and the International Atomic Energy Agency, to achieve the noble goal of realizing this initiative, the implementation of which would seriously enhance international peace and security not only in the Middle East but also beyond.

entries.position_summary: The country strongly supports establishing a Middle East WMD-free zone as part of the 1995 NPT commitments, urging participation by all regional states, specifically calling on the United States and Israel to engage. It criticizes the United States for blocking the 2015 NPT Review Conference final document over language on the zone, signaling concern for the health of the NPT review process.
entries.quote: Certainly, this process can only be meaningful if it involves all Middle East countries excepting no one. In this context, we once again call on the United States, who co-sponsored the 1995 resolution, and Israel not to disregard the Conference, but rather take this opportunity to contribute their vision and engage in an open discussion on strengthening security in the Middle East. We emphasize that we regard the decision on establishing a WMD-free zone in the Middle East as an integral part of the arrangements concerning the NPT reached in 1995. We regret that the United States blocked the final document of the 2015 NPT Review Conference in order to prevent a language on the WMD-free zone.

entries.position_summary: The country insists that UN efforts should not replace the NPT process and that tensions over the Middle East must be addressed within the NPT framework ahead of the 2026 Review Conference. It reaffirms that the 1995 Resolution on the Middle East remains valid until fully implemented.
entries.quote: It is important to emphasize that the UN work is not a substitute for the NPT process. On the way to the 2026 NPT Review Conference, it will be critical to demonstrate that the United Nations forum addresses the tension around the Middle East issue in the context of the NPT review process. We underline once again that the 1995 Resolution on the Middle East remains in force until its full implementation.

entries.position_summary: Russia supports establishing a WMD-free zone in the Middle East and argues it would enhance regional and global stability. It consistently advocates for this zone as a contribution to regional security.
entries.quote: Russia is a consistent supporter of the establishment of a zone free of nuclear and other types of WMD (WMD-free zone) in the Middle East. We are convinced that its creation will contribute to the stability of the Middle East region and globally.

entries.position_summary: Russia recalls co-sponsoring the 1995 NPT Resolution on the Middle East and emphasizes its responsibility to uphold it. It supports keeping the Middle East WMD-free zone on the NPT review agenda until the Resolution’s goals are fully implemented.
entries.quote: Russia was a co-sponsor of the Resolution on the Middle East at the 1995 NPT Review and Extension Conference, which became an important element of the package of decisions that made it possible to ensure the indefinite extension of the Treaty without a vote. We are fully aware of our responsibility for this status and believe that the WMD-free zone issue should remain on the agenda of the NPT review process until the goals and objectives of the 1995 Resolution are fully realized.

entries.position_summary: Russia initially had doubts about the Arab States’ 2018 initiative to place the Middle East WMD-free zone process under UN auspices, indicating early skepticism toward a UN-led approach.
entries.quote: At first, Russia had some doubts about the initiative of Arab States to bring the subject of the WMD-free zone "under the auspices" of the United Nations in 2018.

entries.position_summary: The country supports establishing a Middle East nuclear-weapon-free zone in line with SSOD I and urges states in the region to refrain from nuclear weapons-related activities and place all nuclear activities under IAEA safeguards pending its creation. It contends that Israel’s failure to meet these prerequisites has obstructed the start of substantive negotiations.
entries.quote: 2. The initiative to establish a nuclear-weapon-free zone (NWFZ) was further confirmed by consensus at the tenth special session of the General Assembly (A/S-10/4) in 1978. Moreover, the States Members of the United Nations, within the framework of the then programme of action on disarmament, proposed that “[p]ending the establishment of such a zone in the region, States of the region should … refrain on a reciprocal basis from producing, acquiring or in any other way possessing nuclear weapons and nuclear explosive devices and from permitting the stationing of nuclear weapons on their territory by any third party, and agree to place all their nuclear activities under International Atomic Energy Agency safeguards”.1 Unfortunately, this key requirement of the General Assembly for the start of substantive negotiations has not yet been met by Israel, which has largely predetermined further difficulties in launching the negotiation process.

entries.position_summary: Russia supports establishing a WMD-free zone in the Middle East and will continue contributing as an observer to the UN conference on this initiative. It underscores this as a longstanding non-proliferation priority and references the October 23, 2024 BRICS Kazan declaration in support.
entries.quote: 14. The Russian Federation will continue, commensurate with its role as an observer at the United Nations Conference on a WMD-free zone, to contribute to this crucial task in any way possible. Establishing a WMD-free zone in the Middle East has consistently been one of our country’s foreign policy priorities in the area of non-proliferation. This was also set out in the BRICS Kazan declaration entitled “Strengthening multilateralism for just global development and security”, adopted on 23 October 2024.

entries.position_summary: The country argues that the ME WMDFZ Conference is significantly constrained by the absence of Israel and the United States, implying their participation is necessary for meaningful progress.
entries.quote: Unfortunately, there is a major constraint on the ME WMDFZ Conference process imposed by the absence of Israel and the US among its participants.

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes the United States for not attending the UN Conference on establishing a Middle East WMD-free zone, arguing that the 1995 Middle East Resolution remains in force until fully implemented and that its co-sponsors have ongoing responsibilities.
entries.quote: 11. Every year, the United States has also ignored invitations to the Conference, although the 1995 resolution remains in force until its goals and objectives are fully realized, and the responsibility of its co-sponsors also has no statute of limitations.

entries.position_summary: The country commends the UN Conference’s 2019, 2021, and 2022 sessions on establishing a Middle East WMD-free zone, highlighting the agreed political declaration, consensus-based rules of procedure, and the launch of an intersessional Working Committee. This reflects strong support for the Conference’s process and its consensus decision-making framework.
entries.quote: The results of the three sessions of the UN Conference in New York in 2019, 2021 and 2022 are highly commendable. These include the agreed fundamental political declaration; the approved rules of procedure under which all decisions, both procedural and substantive, are taken by consensus; and the launched inter- sessional interaction in the format of the Working Committee.

entries.position_summary: The country supports the UNGA-launched process to convene annual conferences on establishing a WMD-free zone in the Middle East. It participates as an observer and highlights that substantive discussions and specific decisions have already been achieved.
entries.quote: We welcome the process launched in 2018 by the United Nations General Assembly to convene annual sessions of the Conference on a WMD-free zone in the Middle East, in which our country participates as an observer. Many important substantive discussions have already taken place and a number of specific decisions have been adopted.

entries.position_summary: Russia supports the process to establish a Middle East WMD-free zone by participating as an observer, welcomes other NWS observers, and stresses the importance of engagement by the IAEA Secretariat, OPCW, and the BWC Implementation Support Unit.
entries.quote: Russia participates as an observer in the UN Conference on the Establishment of a Middle East Zone Free of Nuclear Weapons and Other Weapons of Mass Destruction convened in accordance with the UN General Assembly decision 73/546 of 23 December 2018. We welcome the role and contribution of other observers: China, the United Kingdom and France. We believe it important the IAEA Secretariat, the OPCW and the BWC Implementation Support Unit have engaged in this work.

entries.position_summary: The statement criticizes Israel for refusing to participate in the UN conference established by GA decision 73/546 to create a Middle East WMD-free zone. It argues Israel would be protected by consensus rules and could join even as an observer, and faults Israel for isolating itself from key regional security talks.
entries.quote: 10. Regrettably, despite the inclusive nature of General Assembly decision 73/546, Israel continues to refuse to join the process. However, it should be understood that any participant in the Conference is well protected by the rule of consensus on any topics under consideration. Nothing would appear to prevent Tel Aviv from participating in the forum, even initially as an observer. Instead, the Israelis prefer to isolate themselves from the most important Middle East negotiations inextricably linked to regional security, and the only ones involving all States in the region.

entries.position_summary: The country views the UN Conference on a Middle East WMD-free zone as making steady, constructive progress toward outlining a future treaty and remains open to involving states not yet participating. It considers these developments beneficial both within the UN framework and for advancing the issue through the NPT review process.
entries.quote: 13. With regard to the substantive aspects of the United Nations Conference on a WMD-free zone, the negotiation process is ongoing. Despite the unprecedented escalation in the region, the States represented at the forum are not closing the door to the possible involvement of countries not yet participating. The discussions indicate that the Parties are gradually approaching a discussion on the outlines of a future zone treaty. This is beneficial in advancing the establishment of a WMD-free zone, both at the United Nations and through the review process.

entries.position_summary: Russia considers the 1995 Middle East WMD-free zone resolution an integral part of the 1995 NPT package, supports the UNGA-launched annual conference process, and participates as an observer. It stresses that the process must include all Middle Eastern states and urges Israel and the United States to engage in the conference and open discussion.
entries.quote: The issue of establishing a zone free from nuclear weapons and other weapons of mass destruction (WMD-free zone) in the Middle East as envisaged by the resolution of the 1995 NPT Review and Extension Conference, has always played special role in the NPT context. We view this resolution as an integral element of the 1995 NPT agreements. Russia welcomes the process of annual sessions of the Conference on a Middle East WMD-Free zone, which was launched by the UN General Assembly in 2018 and in which our country takes part as an observer. A series of substantive discussions has already taken place, and a number of specific decisions has been made. Certainly, this process can only be meaningful if it involves all Middle East countries excepting no one. We once again call on Israel and the United States not to disregard the Conference and take this opportunity to express their opinion and engage in an open discussion on strengthening security in the Middle East.

entries.position_summary: Supports convening the UN Conference on establishing a Middle East zone free of nuclear and other WMD as a way to advance progress despite NPT deadlock, emphasizing regional ownership by Middle East states. The statement portrays the conference as an effective alternative path to move the process forward.
entries.quote: However, it then became clear that, given the deadlock on the NPT platform, the idea of convening a UN Conference on the Establishment of a Middle East Zone Free of Nuclear Weapons and Other Weapons of Mass Destruction could open an additional window of opportunity to advance the process. And so it turned out to be. In practice, Middle East States took the matter of creating a WMD-free zone into their own hands.

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes the United States for refusing to participate in the UN Conference on establishing a Middle East WMD-free zone and for neglecting its obligations as a co-sponsor of the 1995 Resolution on the Middle East. It accuses Washington of sabotaging efforts to create the zone despite claiming leadership in nonproliferation.
entries.quote: We regret to note that the United States, by its non-participation in the Conference, continues to refrain from its responsibilities as one of the co-sponsors of the 1995 Resolution on the Middle East. We see no real reason for Washington, which, moreover, is constantly trying to claim in words to be a leader in non- proliferation, to further sabotage the meetings aimed at establishing the Middle East WMD-free zone.

entries.position_summary: Russia notes progress toward defining a Middle East WMD-free zone treaty, highlights the need for expert support on its nuclear aspects, and suggests leveraging Vienna’s technical expertise and institutions. It offers its good offices in an observer capacity if regional states request them.
entries.quote: Mr. Chair, Following the results of the three sessions of the United Nations Conference, it is clear that the participants are gradually approaching the discussion of the concrete outlines of the future Treaty on the Middle East WMD-free zone. In this connection, there is a real demand from the States of the region for expert support for the New York discussions, first and foremost with regard to the nuclear aspects of the future zone. We believe that the potential of other United Nations host cities, in particular Vienna, which has the necessary expertise, where discussions take place in a more technical and depoliticized manner and where specialized international organizations are also located, could be of practical benefit in this matter. Within its observer status, the Russian side is ready to provide its good offices if States of the region deem them necessary.

entries.position_summary: The country welcomes the annual conference process launched in 2018 to establish a Middle East zone free of nuclear and other WMD, values its open and inclusive dialogue, and notes Russia’s active participation as an observer. It expresses support for continued engagement in this process.
entries.quote: We welcome the 2018-launched process of annual sessions of the Conference on the Establishment of a Middle East Zone Free of Nuclear Weapons and Other Weapons, in which Russia takes an active part as an observer. It is important that an open and inclusive dialogue has been initiated and that States of

entries.position_summary: Russia supports establishing a WMD-free zone in the Middle East and highlights its own political and diplomatic efforts toward this goal. It notes that the 2010 NPT Review Conference mandated the co-sponsors of the 1995 Middle East resolution and the UN Secretary-General to convene a regional conference by 2012.
entries.quote: 4. The Russian Federation made considerable political and diplomatic efforts to realize the idea of establishing a WMD-free zone. However, it was not until 2010 that the Review Conference mandated the three co-sponsors of the 1995 resolution, together with the Secretary-General, to convene, no later than 2012, a conference on a WMD-free zone with the participation of the States of the region.

entries.position_summary: The statement criticizes the United States for avoiding the Middle East WMD-free zone conference and for applying double standards to regional non-proliferation concerns. It argues this undermines efforts toward a comprehensive regional solution and weakens international peace and security, implying support for advancing the Middle East WMDFZ process.
entries.quote: The efforts of Washington to avoid the Conference do significant damage to the interests of nuclear non-proliferation. It is unacceptable to completely disregard the nuclear non-proliferation concerns of some countries in the region while exaggerating those same concerns for others. Such an approach only distances us from achieving a comprehensive solution to the problems of the Middle East and hinders the strengthening of international peace and security in the region, as envisioned in the Charter of the United Nations.

entries.position_summary: The country supports the dialogue on establishing a Middle East WMD-free zone under consensus-based rules of procedure for both procedural and substantive matters. It views this dialogue as a confidence-building measure that contributes to improving inter-State relations in the region.
entries.quote: 3 whenever it deems it possible, as was elaborated when the forum rules of procedure were agreed: they are based on the principle of consensus on both procedural and substantive issues. The dialogue on the Middle East WMD-free zone, which has an element of confidence building, is inherently incorporated into the positive momentum for improving inter-State relations in the region.

entries.position_summary: The country supports the Conference’s consensus-only decision-making and the creation of an intersessional working committee, seeing these modalities as enabling deeper substantive discussion and building trust among regional states. It views these steps as constructive for advancing a WMD-free zone in the Middle East.
entries.quote: Crucially, the participants have agreed that all decisions of the Conference, whether procedural or substantive, will be taken solely by consensus. It is also significant that the participating States have established an intersessional working committee, which allows for a more substantive and in-depth discussion of issues related to the mandate of the Conference, which in turn plays an important role in building trust among the countries of the region.

entries.position_summary: Russia claims sustained support and contributions toward establishing a WMD-free zone in the Middle East, referencing the 2010 NPT Review Conference Action Plan as a key basis for action.
entries.quote: During these 30 years, Russia has been making significant contribution to the establishment of the WMD-free zone at every stage of this process. One should remember, among other things, the 2010 NPT Review Conference Action Plan and

entries.position_summary: Supports ensuring that Middle Eastern states have a full opportunity to discuss and advance the establishment of a Middle East WMD-free zone.
entries.quote: 7 the Middle East have a full opportunity to discuss ways towards establishing the zone.

entries.position_summary: The country argues that NATO states politicize the NPT by asserting unilateral interpretations to pressure others, which undermines the review process. It criticizes the 2015 and 2022 review dynamics, including the exclusion of Middle East WMDFZ language, and appears to support stronger, depoliticized treatment of the Middle East zone in outcomes.
entries.quote: The excessive politicization of work within the NPT should be noted. In recent decades, a certain group of countries - more precisely, the member States of the NATO nuclear bloc and their allies - have usurped the right to interpret States' compliance with their obligations under the Treaty and have used its provisions to pressure undesired States and justify interference in their internal affairs. This approach has also had a negative impact on the review process: during the 2015 Conference, some NPT parties deemed passages on a Middle East Weapons of Mass Destruction Free Zone inappropriate for an outcome document; and in 2022, a group of States found it possible to promote controversial and obviously

entries.position_summary: Urges the United States to join the Middle East WMD-Free Zone negotiation process as an observer, following the example of Russia, China, the United Kingdom and France.
entries.quote: 12. The United States should carefully analyse the situation and make the only correct decision by joining the negotiation process as an observer, as the Russian Federation, China, the United Kingdom and France have done.
